data.title: M-Up Holdings (TSE:3661) Earnings Growth Of 66.4% Reinforces Bullish Narratives
data.description:
m-up holdings (TSE:3661) has just put out its Q3 2026 scorecard, with recent quarterly figures showing revenue moving from ¥6.8b in Q4 2025 to ¥7.4b in Q1 2026 and ¥7.7b in Q2 2026, alongside basic EPS of ¥12.07 and ¥11.84 in Q1 and Q2 respectively and trailing 12 month earnings growth of 66.4%. Over the past few reported periods, revenue has shifted from ¥6.7b in Q3 2025 to ¥7.7b in Q2 2026, while basic EPS has moved from ¥11.48 to ¥11.84 over the same quarters. This gives investors a clear view of how the top and bottom lines have been tracking into this latest release. With the trailing net margin sitting at 8.8% versus 6.9% a year earlier, this set of results keeps the focus squarely on how efficiently m-up holdings is turning its sales into profit.

See our full analysis for m-up holdings.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the key stories investors have been telling about m-up holdings, and how the latest margin trends might prompt those narratives to adjust.

66.4% earnings growth backs high quality label

  • Over the last 12 months, net income was ¥2,529 million on revenue of ¥28,623 million, which supports the 66.4% earnings growth figure tied to m-up holdings over that period.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that this 66.4% trailing earnings growth sits alongside earnings described as high quality, supported by quarterly net income ranging from ¥819 million to ¥859 million in Q3 2025, Q1 2026, and Q2 2026. This points to a run of sizeable profit contributions rather than a single one off spike.
    • Bulls also point to the 5 year net earnings growth rate of 24.9% per year, and the trailing 12 month net margin of 8.8% versus 6.9% a year earlier, as evidence that profit growth is not just a short term event.
    • The quarterly pattern, with revenue moving from ¥6,725 million in Q3 2025 to ¥7,694 million in Q2 2026 while net income held around the ¥800 million level in three different quarters, gives bulls some support for the idea that the business has been able to produce consistent earnings across multiple reporting periods.

Bulls argue that the combination of high quality earnings, 66.4% trailing growth, and multi year expansion in profits is exactly the kind of profile that can sustain investor interest when shorter term headlines fade. 🐂 m-up holdings Bull Case

8.8% net margin and EPS resilience

  • The trailing net margin of 8.8% comes from ¥2,529 million of net income on ¥28,623 million of revenue, and within that, Q1 2026 and Q2 2026 basic EPS of ¥12.07 and ¥11.84 sit close to the Q3 2025 level of ¥11.48 despite quarterly revenue volumes ranging from ¥6,725 million to ¥7,694 million.
  • Supporters of a bullish narrative highlight that this 8.8% margin alongside mid teens EPS figures in most quarters sits in contrast to Q4 2025, where EPS dropped to ¥0.15 on revenue of ¥6,791 million. This suggests that the stronger recent quarters are not just driven by sales size but also by the earnings profile of the underlying operations.
    • Quarterly revenue moved from ¥5,837 million in Q1 2025 to ¥7,694 million in Q2 2026, while net income moved from ¥476 million to ¥840 million across those two points, so bulls see a relationship between higher scale and the current margin level.
    • At the same time, critics focused on that very weak Q4 2025 result may see it as a counterpoint to the bullish argument, yet the subsequent Q1 2026 and Q2 2026 profits bring the trailing numbers back in line with the 8.8% margin that investors are using to frame current profitability.

P/E of 22.1x versus 35.3% DCF gap

  • At a share price of ¥787, the stock trades on a P/E of 22.1x, compared with a JP Software industry average of 17.3x and a peer average of 50.2x, while the latest DCF fair value is ¥1,215.94, implying the shares are about 35.3% below that DCF figure.
  • General market opinion has to balance a bullish style claim that the current price is well below DCF fair value with a more cautious angle that the P/E is higher than the broader industry, and the numbers give weight to both sides.
    • On one hand, the 35.3% gap between the ¥787 share price and the ¥1,215.94 DCF fair value, together with 66.4% trailing earnings growth, is often used by optimistic investors as support for a value and growth combination argument.
    • On the other hand, the 22.1x P/E relative to the 17.3x industry average gives more cautious investors a reason to question how much of that trailing growth and margin profile is already being reflected in the current market multiple even if the P/E is below the 50

data.title: Is Goldman Sachs Group (GS) Pricing Still Justified After Strong Multi Year Share Gains
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Goldman Sachs Group is still attractively priced after its recent run, you are not alone. This article will walk through what the current share price might be implying about value.
  • The stock last closed at US$905.14, with returns of 39.8% over 1 year, 164.8% over 3 years and 223.0% over 5 years. The past month and year to date show declines of 5.9% and 1.0%, and there has been a 2.5% decline over the last week.
  • Recent market attention around large US financial institutions and ongoing discussion of capital requirements and industry regulation has kept Goldman Sachs Group in focus. These themes help frame how investors think about risk, growth potential and what they are willing to pay for bank and investment banking names.
  • On our checks, Goldman Sachs Group scores 3 out of 6 on valuation. This means it screens as undervalued on half of the metrics we track. Next we will look at what different valuation approaches say about that price, before finishing with a way to tie them together into a clearer view of value.

Goldman Sachs Group delivered 39.8% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Capital Markets industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Goldman Sachs Group Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit Goldman Sachs Group can generate above the return that equity investors are assumed to require, then capitalizes that surplus to estimate what the shares might be worth today.

For Goldman Sachs Group, analysts estimate a stable earnings figure of $67.32 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity forecasts from 14 analysts. The cost of equity is put at $49.61 per share, which implies an excess return of $17.72 per share. In other words, the model assumes earnings are comfortably above the required return that shareholders expect.

The model also uses a current book value of $357.46 per share and a stable book value estimate of $402.33 per share, again based on analyst inputs, together with an average Return on Equity of 16.73%. These inputs are combined to produce an intrinsic value estimate of about $600.95 per share.

Compared with the recent share price of $905.14, this Excess Returns valuation implies the stock is about 50.6% overvalued.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Goldman Sachs Group may be overvalued by 50.6%. Discover 53 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Goldman Sachs Group.

Approach 2: Goldman Sachs Group Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Goldman Sachs Group, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings that share currently generates. It gives you a quick sense of how many dollars investors are willing to pay today for one dollar of earnings.

What counts as a "normal" or "fair" P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth prospects and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk usually line up with a lower multiple.

Goldman Sachs Group currently trades on a P/E of 17.42x. That compares with a Capital Markets industry average P/E of 23.12x and a peer group average of 26.66x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework goes a step further and estimates what a more tailored P/E might look like based on factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market value and company specific risks. For Goldman Sachs Group, the Fair Ratio is 19.98x, which is higher than the current 17.42x, suggesting the shares are trading below this customized benchmark.

Result: UNDERVALUED

🐂 Goldman Sachs Group Bull Case

  • They see efficiency gains from AI and digital tools, along with capital light financing businesses, as helping returns on equity and supporting flexible capital returns.

Eutelsat Communications (ENXTPA:ETL) H1 2026 Results: Losses Persist as Revenue Holds Just Below Prior Levels

Eutelsat Communications (ENXTPA:ETL) has reported H1 2026 results with total revenue of €637.5 million and a basic EPS loss of €0.44, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of €1.23 billion and a basic EPS loss of €0.85. The company has seen revenue move from €640.4 million in H2 2024 to €606.2 million in H1 2025 and then to €637.5 million in H2 2025, while basic EPS has stayed in loss territory over the same sequence, ranging from a €0.25 loss per share in H2 2024 to a €1.84 loss in H1 2025 and a €0.44 loss in H2 2025. This keeps the focus firmly on earnings quality and margin pressure for investors. With the shares trading around €2.24 and losses still weighing on the income statement, the key question now is how much operating leverage and margin repair the business can realistically deliver over time.

See our full analysis for Eutelsat Communications.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the prevailing market and community narratives to see which views on Eutelsat hold up and which might need a rethink.

See what the community is saying about Eutelsat Communications

Losses Narrow To €209 Million On Similar Revenue

  • Net income loss for H2 2025 came in at €208.7 million on €637.5 million of revenue, compared with a €873.2 million loss on €606.2 million of revenue in H1 2025, while trailing 12 month losses sit at €445.2 million on €1.23b of revenue.
  • Bulls point out that LEO and government connectivity are intended to support margin recovery over time. However, the trailing 12 month loss of €445.2 million still sits against revenue of €1.23b, which:
    • Heavily contrasts with the bullish idea of future EBITDA margins of at least 60%, because current earnings are clearly far from that kind of profitability.
    • Suggests any improvement case rests on a shift in mix toward LEO and government contracts, while today’s reported figures are still dominated by loss making operations.

Big swings in profit and loss like this can make it hard to judge the longer term story just from one half year set of numbers. This is why bulls keep pointing back to the LEO roll out and contract pipeline as the core of their argument. 🐂 Eutelsat Communications Bull Case

Price To Sales Premium With Deep DCF Discount

  • At a share price of €2.24 and trailing 12 month revenue of €1.23b, Eutelsat trades on a P/S of about 0.9x, compared with peers at 0.6x and the French Media industry at 0.5x, even though the supplied DCF fair value of €9.24 sits very far above the current price.
  • Bears argue that paying a premium P/S multiple for an unprofitable company is risky, and the data gives them some support, even with the low price against DCF:
    • Losses over the past five years have grown at roughly 67.7% per year and the company is expected to remain loss making over the next three years, which does not obviously line up with a higher multiple than peers.
    • The same dataset flags elevated share price volatility over the last three months, so anyone focusing on that 0.9x P/S versus lower peer multiples may see the DCF fair value gap as less convincing without clearer progress toward profitability.

For you as a shareholder or potential investor, the tension here is that the model based DCF fair value of €9.24 suggests a large upside gap, while the premium P/S and continuing losses give bears plenty to point at. 🐻 Eutelsat Communications Bear Case

Revenue Growth Forecast Trails French Market

  • The current dataset shows Eutelsat’s revenue forecast at about 4.6% annual growth over the next few years, compared with a 5.7% forecast for the wider French market, while EPS is expected to stay negative over that period.
  • Consensus style views often lean on LEO projects, government contracts and reduced GEO CapEx to support the long term case, and the numbers partly align but also leave questions:
    • Forecast revenue growth below the broader French market, despite all the LEO investment, means the growth profile is not especially fast compared with the benchmark that many investors use.
    • The fact that forecasts still show negative EPS over the next three years sits awkwardly with analyst targets that assume Eutelsat eventually reaches profit margins similar to the GB Media industry.

Approach 1: Centrus Energy Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those amounts back to today, using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in present terms.

For Centrus Energy, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows available to shareholders. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $41.6 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections show Free Cash Flow moving through a mix of negative and positive years, with a projected $176 million in 2030, all expressed in US$.

Bringing these projected cash flows back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $214.66 per share. Compared with the current share price of around $199, the model implies the stock is about 7.2% undervalued. This is a relatively small gap and suggests the market price is fairly close to this DCF estimate.

Approach 2: Centrus Energy Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties directly to the bottom line and lets you compare how the market prices those earnings across different businesses.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk usually point to a lower, more conservative multiple.

Centrus Energy currently trades on a P/E of about 50.35x. That is above the Oil and Gas industry average of around 14.46x and also higher than the peer group average of roughly 44.06x. Simply Wall St’s “Fair Ratio” for Centrus Energy is 13.10x. This Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that might be reasonable given factors such as earnings growth characteristics, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks.

Compared with simple peer or industry comparisons, the Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored, because it adjusts for those company specific factors instead of assuming all businesses deserve similar multiples. data.description:

The company has seen quarterly revenue move from €416.5 million in Q2 2024 to €435.6 million in Q3 2025, with EPS stepping from €0.49 to €0.70 over the same run, giving investors a clear view of how the top and bottom lines are tracking through the year. With a trailing net margin of 8.6% and the share price around €44.58, the latest results keep the focus squarely on how durable these margins look from here.

With the numbers on the table, the next step is to see how they stack up against the prevailing stories around Kalmar, and which of those narratives hold up when you line them against the recent trends in growth and profitability.

Margins Steady Around 8.6% Net

  • Over the last 12 months, Kalmar’s net income of €145 million on €1.7b of revenue works out to an 8.6% net margin, slightly above the 8.1% margin reported a year earlier.
  • Bulls point out that this margin profile lines up with several supportive trends, yet the numbers still leave room for debate:
    • On the bullish side, services now carry an 18.5% margin and 34% of sales, and programs like Driving Excellence are targeting €50 million in annualised efficiencies. Investors arguing the bullish case see this as helpful for sustaining or lifting margins.
    • At the same time, tariffs on U.S. bound equipment and batteries plus price increases of 5% to 15% create the risk that cost pressure or customer pushback eventually eats into that 8.6% net level. This gives bears something concrete to point to when they question how durable these margins are.
Kalmar’s recent margin print is what bullish investors often like to see before talking about higher earnings potential, yet the tariff and pricing backdrop means the story is far from one sided. Earnings Trend Breaks From 5 Year Decline

  • Trailing earnings growth over the last year is 0.5%, compared with a 13.8% per year decline over the past five years, and Q3 net income of €44.7 million sits above the €31.2 million recorded in Q2 2024.
  • Bears argue that a single year of modest growth does not fully answer questions raised by that multi year decline, and the quarterly pattern gives them some data to work with:
    • Q3 2025 earnings per share of €0.70 follow earlier quarters of €0.53 and €0.61 in 2025, but the longer term record still shows that earnings had been shrinking at 13.8% per year. Cautious investors highlight this as evidence that profitability has not yet shown a long multi year rebuild.
    • Even with a DCF fair value of €57.50 and a current price of €44.58, skeptics note that a 0.5% trailing earnings lift on its own is a small shift compared with the past erosion. They look for more than a few quarters of improvement before they fully buy into a lasting turnaround.
That mix of a small positive earnings step against a long history of declines is why more cautious investors still push the bearish angle, even as the recent numbers start to move in a direction they prefer. 🐻 Kalmar Oyj Bear Case

P/E Discount Versus Peers Stands Out

  • Kalmar trades on a trailing P/E of 19.7x at a share price of €44.58, compared with a peer average of 22.5x and a European Machinery industry average of 21.6x, while the DCF fair value is set at €57.50.

    • On the other, the five year earnings decline of 13.8% per year provides a concrete counterpoint, as more cautious investors see the lower P/E multiple as a reflection of that track record rather than a clear signal that the market has overlooked something material.
